content.description:
## Context

View code changes now live-reload (rendering system watches `byViewId` and bypasses selector filtering when the view itself changed). But library changes still require a page refresh — updating a library item doesn't trigger re-renders of views that use it.

## Why Hob Makes This Easier

**Fresh evaluation eliminates stale references.** Hob views re-evaluate from scratch on each render. Every `(require "name")` call executes again, so if the module cache is invalidated, the next render automatically picks up new library code. In JS, views capture library exports in closures at load time — even after cache invalidation, existing closures still hold old references.

**The interpreter can track dependencies.** The Hob interpreter controls execution, so `require` calls during eval can be intercepted to build a dependency graph: "this render instance depends on libraries X, Y, Z." When library X is updated, the system knows exactly which instances to re-render. This is the same pattern as `get-item :select` — the interpreter already tracks which items a render depends on.

## Implementation Sketch

1. During Hob eval, each `(require "name")` registers the library's item ID as a dependency on the current render instance (analogous to how `get-item` registers item dependencies)
2. On `item:updated` for a library, look up dependent instances and schedule re-renders
3. Invalidate the module cache entry for that library so re-eval gets fresh code
4. Bypass selector filtering for library changes (same as view changes)

## Design Question

Should library dependencies be tracked per-instance (like selectors) or globally? Per-instance is more precise but global may be fine in practice — libraries rarely change, and when they do you probably want everything using them to refresh.

## JS Views

Harder for JS views because closures capture library exports at module load time. Even with cache invalidation, existing JS view code still references old exports. Full re-render (re-evaluating the JS module from scratch) would be needed, which is effectively what Hob already does naturally.
